blocks.content: The first loop through the carnival had the quality of group happiness at its most specific: not the broad happiness of good fortune but the narrow, particular happiness of these five people in this place on this afternoon.

Nathan's ring toss attempt was extended and theatrical and unsuccessful in a way that generated considerable commentary from Maya, who had not attempted the ring toss but had very specific opinions about his technique.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: The rings are circular. You're throwing at an angle.

Ethan and Cassandra watched this from a few feet back, eating kettle corn out of a shared bag. There was something about the rhythm of it — the reaching into the bag, the casual proximity — that had the quality of a long established habit even though it was new. Things between them sometimes felt like that. Like echoes of a future that had already been decided.
She laughed at something Nathan said — the unguarded laugh, the one that happened before she decided to laugh — and Ethan watched it and thought what he always thought when it arrived: there it is.
He ate his kettle corn. He was here.
